coredata.dc:title: Folic acid conjugated capecitabine capped green synthesized fluorescent carbon dots as a targeted nano-delivery system for colorectal cancer
coredata.dc:creator: Paliwal, Shivangi
Jana, Pradip
Singh, Sneha
Madhyastha, Harishkumar
Webster, Thomas J.
Dev, Abhimanyu
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we have developed folic acid conjugated capecitabine capped passivated carbon dots (CAP-FA-CDPs) for colorectal cancer targeting. Carbon dots were synthesized via a microwave treatment of the Buchnania lanzan leaf extract and PEG6000 was used as a surface passivating agent. The developed system has shown an acidic pH (pH 5.0) dependent drug release of 84.57% after 72 h. In an in vitro cytotoxicity study, CDPs showed almost no toxicity to the HT-29 colorectal cancer cells with the viability rate of 96.03%. However, the viability of HT-29 cells significantly dropped to 24.4% in CAP-FA-CDPs treated group, only after 36 h of incubation. A cellular uptake study showed an efficient internalization of CAP-FA-CDPs systems by HT-29 cells expected via folic acid-folate receptor (ligand-receptor) interaction. Greater reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation further confirmed the successful cellular uptake of the CAP-FA-CDPs. Finally, flow cytometry analysis clearly showed the apoptotic potential of the CAP-FA-CDPs conjugated system on HT-29 cells where live cells significantly decreased to 21.4% and apoptotic cells increased to 77.2%. Thus, the presently developed CAP-FA-CDPs nano delivery system offers a promising strategy for targeted and effective drug delivery in colorectal cancer.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Capecitabine
Carbon dots
Colorectal cancer
Folate receptor
Folic acid
Targeted drug delivery

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CDPs, FA-CDPs and CAP-FA-CDPs
Preparation of the leaf extract of B. lanzan
Synthesis of CDs and passivated CDs (CDPs) from the leaf extract
Characterization of the synthesized carbon dots
CAP loading efficiency and loading content

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Pradip Jana would like to acknowledge Indian Council of Medical Research ( ICMR ), New Delhi, India for providing a Senior Research Fellowship (SRF) (Award letter file no. 5/3/8/26/ITR-F/2022 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence of CDs and CDPs in (a) day light, (b) short UV light and (c) long UV light.

PL excitation and PL emission spectra of CDPs. (A) Excitation spectrum with λmax at 282 nm, (B) Emission spectrum with λmax at 325 nm, and (C) Emission spectra of CDPs at different excitation wavelengths.

UV–visible absorption spectra of: (A) Passivated carbon dots, (CDPs), (B) Capecitabine (CAP), (C) Folic acid (FA), (D) Capecitabine loaded onto folic acid conjugated passivated carbon dots (CAP-FA-CDPs), and (E) Combined spectra of CDPs, CAP, FA and CAP-FA-CDPs.
FTIR spectrum of (A) CDPs, (B) FA-CDPs, (C) CAP-FA-CDPs; (D) combined spectra of CDs, CDPs, FA-CDPs and CAP-FA-CDPs.
XPS survey of (A) CDPs, (B) FA-CDPs and (C) CAP-FA-CDPs.

TEM images (left side) and EDX spectra (right side) of (A) passivated carbon dots (CDPs), (B) folic acid conjugated carbon dots (FA-CDPs), and (C) Capecitabine loaded-folic acid conjugated passivated carbon dots (CAP-FA-CDPs). Scale bar in TEM images: 50 nm.
XRD patterns of passivated carbon dots (CDPs) and capecitabine loaded folic acid conjugated passivated carbon dots (CAP-FA-CDPs).
Optical contact angles of (A) CDPs, (B) FA-CDPs and (C) CAP-FA-CDPs.

(A) Release profile of CAP from a CAP-FA-CDPs conjugated system at pH 5.0, 6.8 and 7.4 and (B) Statistical analysis of cumulative drug release (%) from the CAP-FA-CDPs conjugated system at pH 5.0, 6.8 and 7.4 on different time intervals (0–72 h).

Cytotoxicity profile of CAP, CDPs, FA-CDPs and CAP-FA-CDPs on HT-29 cancer cells: (A) % cell viability at different concentrations (0.05 −1.0 μg/mL) and (B) % cell viability at different time intervals (2–36 h). The statistically significant difference between the cells treated with CAP-FA-CDPs and CDPs is represented by * (p < 0.05; n = 3) and * ** (p < 0.001; n = 3). Statistical analysis of % cell viability of CAP-FA-CDP at different time points (one way ANOVA- Bonferroni post-test) was also carried out. Significant difference was seen (p < 0.0001; n = 3) when % cell viability at 2 h was compared to 36 h.

ROS study results. Fluorescence intensity exhibited by HT-29 cells when treated with CAP, CDPs, FA-CDPs and CAP-FA-CDPs at different concentrations. The statistically significant difference between the cells treated with CDPs and CAP-FA-CDPs is represented by * ** (p < 0.001; n = 3).

Confocal microscopic images of HT-29 cells, where the developed fluorescence reflects the level of ROS production. (A) control image, (B) image of CAP treated cells (0.1 µg/mL), (C) image of CDPs treated cells (0.1 µg/mL), (D) image of FA-CDPs treated cells (0.1 µg/mL), and (E) image of CAP-FA-CDPs treated cells (0.1 µg/mL).
Fluorescence images showing cellular uptake into HT-29 cells treated with. (A) Capecitabine loaded folic acid conjugated passivated carbon dots (CAP-FA-CDPs) and (B) passivated carbon dots (CDPs).
Images showing the results of flow cytometry analysis of the Wild type, CDPs, FA-CDPs, CAP and CAP-FA-CDPs treated HT-29 cells.
Synthesis of CDs from the Buchnania lanzan leaf extract, passivation of CDs and surface attachment of folic acid (FA) and capecitabine (CAP) to form CAP-FA-CDPs.
Table showing the equation and R2 value of the various models.
Comparison of IC50 among earlier reported nano formulations and CAP-FA-CDPs in colon cancer cell line.

row.entry: Chitosan glucuronic acid conjugate coated CAP loaded mesoporous silica nanoparticles
HCT-116 colon cancer cell line, IC50 = 117.3 ± 5.92 µM (42.04 µg/mL)
HT-29 colon cancer cell line, IC50 = 52 µg/mL (approx.)
Folic acid conjugated CAP loaded CDPs (CAP-FA-CDPs)
HT-29 colon cancer cell line, IC50 = 0.73µg/mL

head.ce:title: Folic acid conjugated capecitabine capped green synthesized fluorescent carbon dots as a targeted nano-delivery system for colorectal cancer

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Pharmaceutical Sciences & Technology, Birla Institute of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we have developed folic acid conjugated capecitabine capped passivated carbon dots (CAP-FA-CDPs) for colorectal cancer targeting. Carbon dots were synthesized via a microwave treatment of the Buchnania lanzan leaf extract and PEG6000 was used as a surface passivating agent. The developed system has shown an acidic pH (pH 5.0) dependent drug release of 84.57% after 72 h. In an in vitro cytotoxicity study, CDPs showed almost no toxicity to the HT-29 colorectal cancer cells with the viability rate of 96.03%. However, the viability of HT-29 cells significantly dropped to 24.4% in CAP-FA-CDPs treated group, only after 36 h of incubation. A cellular uptake study showed an efficient internalization of CAP-FA-CDPs systems by HT-29 cells expected via folic acid-folate receptor (ligand-receptor) interaction. Greater reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation further confirmed the successful cellular uptake of the CAP-FA-CDPs. Finally, flow cytometry analysis clearly showed the apoptotic potential of the CAP-FA-CDPs conjugated system on HT-29 cells where live cells significantly decreased to 21.4% and apoptotic cells increased to 77.2%. Thus, the presently developed CAP-FA-CDPs nano delivery system offers a promising strategy for targeted and effective drug delivery in colorectal cancer.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is a chronic health problem affecting the entire world today [1]. It is the second most prevalent reason for disease related deaths across the globe [2]. Among all types of cancer, colorectal cancer (CRC) is the third most common cancer and second most frequent cause of cancer related deaths worldwide. CRC disrupts the lives of approximately 1.8 million patients and causes 881,000 fatalities, as published in the Globocan International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC) reports in 2018 [3]. Chemotherapy still remains one of the most common and effective treatment strategies for cancer in which tumors are eliminated by direct cytotoxic effects [4]. However, in chemotherapy there is a lot of concerns regarding poor therapeutic effects and severe side effects of small molecule drugs due to their non-specific accumulation in tumor tissues [5,6] .

Capecitabine (CAP), an oral prodrug of 5-fluorouracil, is widely used to treat gastrointestinal cancers either as a monotherapy or in combination with other chemotherapeutics. But whole-body drug distribution because of non-specific delivery, requirement of high doses and repeated administration for effective therapy lead to adverse effects like diarrhoea, alopecia, liver malfunction, stomatitis, hand-foot syndrome and fatigue [7–9] . Among these, the hand-foot syndrome limits the dosage of CAP, as reported in more than 50% of patients. At present, there is no well-established management strategy for this reaction other than the temporary discontinuation of chemotherapy until the healing of skin lesions has occurred and afterwards, resumption of therapy with a reduced dose. [10]. These significant issues in colorectal cancer treatment can be tackled using a targeted drug delivery system that minimizes drug distribution to the non-target areas and, thereby, reduces related toxic effects and improves therapeutic efficacy [11].

Over the last decade the emergence of nanomaterials in cancer targeted drug delivery have shown promising outcomes as they can deliver drugs to the target site, increase utilization of drugs, improve curative effect and reduce adverse effects. Various nanomaterials such as liposomes, dendrimers, polymeric nanoparticles, iron oxide nanoparticles, mesoporous silica nanoparticles, gold nanoparticles, quantum dots, carbon nanoparticles, etc. have been utilized as a carrier to deliver anticancer drugs [5,12]. Ameli et al. developed a nano delivery system using cyclodextrin and copolymer based on a pH responsive micelle for CAP delivery and release in colon cancer [13]. Patel et al. formulated CAP loaded niosomes [14], Dudhipala et al. developed CAP loaded solid lipid nanoparticles and evaluated against HT-29 cells [15], Rajasree et al. prepared eudragitR S100 encapsulated cationic poly (lactic-co-glycolic acid) based nanoparticles for specific CAP delivery in colorectal cancer [16]. Recently, Dominguez et al. synthesized single walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) and covalently linked fluorescein (a fluorophore) and folic acid (a targeting molecule) for detection and targeting, respectively, of colorectal cancer. Further, they grafted CAP to the SWCNTs surface and tested against human colon cancer (Caco 2) cells where the antiproliferative effect of synthesized complex was found much superior as compared to CAP alone [17].

In recent times, carbon based quantum dots or simply carbon dots (CDs), a fascinating carbon nanoparticle with a size smaller than 10 nm has been widely considered in the drug delivery field due to their good water solubility, excellent chemical inertness, minimal toxicity and good biocompatibility [18,19]. Furthermore, CDs has emerged as a novel carrier material for tracking and targeting of chemotherapeutic drugs due to their strong photoluminescence (PL) and ease of surface functionalization [20–23]. Several synthesis methods of CDs have been reported but they generally requre expensive and toxic starting materials, tedious and high temperature and complex process. Thus, the green synthesis approach having the advantages including use of nontoxic chemicals and solvents, simple, economical and environment friendly process, use of renewable resources is getting much attention [24,25]. Wide natural sources like milk, honey, orange juice and other kitchen waste materials have been explored for the synthesis of CDs having suitable fluorescence and surface properties [26–29].

Folate receptors (FRs) are well recognized as highly expressed targets in cancer cells, whereas their expression is minimal in normal cells [30,31]. Folic acid (FA) is a commonly used ligand for cancer cell targeting due to its strong affinity towards FR [32,33]. Utilizing this exclusive characteristic, a FA conjugated carrier system could be efficiently taken-up by cancer cells via a FR mediated endocytosis process [34]. Recently, Zamani et al. developed a polymeric nanocarrier for the delivery of tamoxifen and quercetin in cancer cells using folic acid as a targeting ligand [35]. In a similar way, Zhang et al. used folic acid as a targeting ligand and conjugated with green luminescent carbon dots to develop a nanoprobe for the detection of FR positive cancer cells. They reported successful entry of FA-CDs into cancer cells via receptor mediated endocytosis and the ability of the developed system to differentiate FR-positive HepG2 cancer cells from normal cells [36].

Thus, in the present work, we have designed folic acid (FA) conjugated CAP capped passivated carbon dots (CAP-FA-CDPs) as an active targeting strategy for colorectal cancer ( Scheme 1). CDs was prepared by a simple microwave treatment of the methanolic extract of leaf of the plant Buchnania lanzan (BL) as a green synthesis approach. The methanolic extract of Buchnania lanzan leaf has been reported to be rich in carbohydrate constituents which serves as the carbon precursor [37,38]. The surface passivation of the prepared CDs was done using polyethylene glycol (PEG6000) to enhance its photoluminescence property. A series of characterizations were performed to determine the optical, structural and morphological properties of the obtained CDPs, FA-CDPs and CAP-FA-CDPs. The fluorescence behavior of CDPs was studied using fluorescence spectroscopy. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) studies revealed structural attributes, whereas Transmission electron microscope (TEM) determined the particle size. Hydrophilicity and crystallinity of the prepared CDPs were evaluated via optical contact angles and X-ray diffraction (XRD), respectively. An in vitro drug release study was also conducted at three different pH values of 5.0, 6.8 and 7.4. In vitro cell viability, ROS, cellular uptake, and apoptotic studies were carried out using a human colon adenocarcinoma WiDr (HT-29) cell line.

CAP was obtained from Hetero Labs Ltd., Hyderabad, India as a gift sample. All reagents and solvents used were of analytical grade, purchased either from Hi-Media or Sigma-Aldrich. Human colon adenocarcinoma WiDr (HT-29) cells were purchased from the National Institute of Biomedical Innovation, Health and Nutrition (NIBIOHN, Ibaraki, Osaka, Japan) for an in vitro cell line study. Reactive oxygen species (ROS) generated intracellularly was determined using an OxiSelect™ Intracellular ROS Assay Kit (DoJindo, Inc, Washington, DC, USA). A fluorescein isothiocyaninate (FITC) Annexin V apoptosis evaluation kit was purchased from Nacalai Tesque, Tokyo, Japan. Dialysis bags (molecular weight cut-off (MWCO), 12–14 kDa) were purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDPs, FA-CDPs and CAP-FA-CDPs
Preparation of the leaf extract of B. lanzan
ce:section.ce:para: The leaves of Buchnania lanzan were thoroughly washed under tap water and then with Milli-Q water to remove all of the unwanted foreign materials and dust particles. After washing and removal of its mid-rib, the leaves were cut into small pieces and were soaked in methanol overnight for cold maceration. The leaves were further made into a paste-like consistency with the aid of a mortar and pestle. The macerated leaves were then filtered to obtain the extract and kept at room temperature for synthesis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs and passivated CDs (CDPs) from the leaf extract
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs were synthesized by a microwave method with a LG microwave (750 W) following previously reported works [39–41] with required modifications. Till date, the actual mechanism of CDs formation is not clear. However, some research groups reported that the reaction mechanism involves initial conversion of precursor molecule into fluorophores under heating and further heating causes polymerization followed by carbonization which ultimately lead to the formation of CDs [42]. In the current work, briefly, 30 mL of the methanolic extract of BL leaf was placed in a beaker and treated in a microwave oven for around 8 min continuously. The obtained residue was cooled at room temperature and then reconstituted with an equal volume of Milli-Q water and was again treated in a microwave oven for approximately 30 min. The acquired residue was reconstituted with 30 mL of Milli-Q water and the resulting solution was filtered with the help of a 0.22 µm syringe filter. Further, the filtrate was lyophilized to obtain a dry powder. For passivated CDs, the residue obtained after methanol evaporation was reconstituted with a PEG6000 solution. The PEG6000 solution was prepared by the admixture of 500 mg of PEG6000 in 30 mL of Milli-Q water. The reconstituted solution was then again treated in a microwave oven for 30 min. Afterwards, the obtained residue was dissolved in 30 mL of Milli-Q water and filtered through a syringe filter. Finally, the filtrate was lyophilized to acquire dry CDPs powder.

FA conjugation to the CDPs was achieved by a sonication method. Briefly, 11 mg of FA was mixed with 5 mL of water. To this mixture, 20 μL of 1 M NaOH was added and magnetically stirred until a color change was observed from a turbid yellow to a transparent yellow solution. To this solution, 5 mg of CDPs was added and it was probe sonicated for 15 min at a 100% amplitude. The resulting mixture was then dialyzed against deionized water with the aid of a dialysis bag (MWCO 12–14 kDa) until free FA was dialyzed out of the mixture. The mixture was then lyophilized to obtain a dry product.

For the loading of CAP onto the FA-CDPs, 1 mL of FA-CDPs in Milli-Q water (250 μg/mL) and 1 mL of CAP in phosphate buffer saline (PBS) (pH 7.4) (8.33 μg/mL) were mixed with 2 mL of PBS (pH 7.4) while stirring continuously for 24 h at 200 rpm shaking speed and 24 °C in an incubator shaker. The resulting solution was dialyzed using dialysis tubing of MWCO (12–14 kDa) against 40 mL of deionized water for 3 h to eliminate any unreacted FA-CDPs and CAP. The UV absorbance of unbound CAP was measured at a 303 nm wavelength. The amount of unbound CAP was determined using a standard curve for CAP. Further, the drug loading efficiency (DLE) and drug loading content (DLC) were determined. The dialyzed solution was lyophilized to obtain a dry powder of CAP-FA-CDPs.

The fluorescence emission spectra of CDPs were acquired using a Spectramax multimode plate reader at excitation wavelengths of 280–350 nm, with an increase of 10 nm. The ultraviolet (UV)-visible absorption spectra of the materials of interest were obtained using a Shimadzu 2450 UV-Visible spectrophotometer. FTIR spectra were recorded using a Shimadzu 8400 S spectrophotometer. The test samples were analyzed implementing the KBr pellet method. Samples were grinded with KBr and discs were prepared for analysis. For elemental analysis, CDPs, FA-CDPs and CAP-FA-CDPs were crushed to form powders and XPS was performed. The particle size of CDPs, FA-CDPs and CAP-FA-CDPs were determined using a TEM functioning at 220 kV voltage. Energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis also revealed the expected elements. CDP crystallinity was analyzed using XRD at a high concentration by spreading on a glass slide. Zeta potential was measured by a Malvern Zetasizer. Optical contact angles of the prepared CDPs were measured by OCAH230.

An in vitro drug release study of the CAP-FA-CDPs solution was performed at three different pH values, i.e., 5.0, 6.8 and 7.4. The drug release process was carried out using an incubator shaker at 200 rpm and 24 °C in the dark. After fixed time intervals, the samples were withdrawn and refilled with the same volume of fresh phosphate buffer saline. UV absorbance of the withdrawn aliquots were then recorded to calculate the released amount of CAP from the CAP-FA-CDPs conjugate.

HT-29 cells were cultured in Dulbecco`s modified eagles’ medium in the presence of 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS). Cells were cultured in 1% v/v PSN of a combination of penicillin (5 mg/mL), streptomycin (5 mg/mL), and neomycin (10 mg/mL) under humidified conditions of 5% CO2 at 37 °C. Cells having a passage number of 2–5 were selected for the in vitro toxicity assay. Cells at a stage of semi confluence were then used to test different treatments at different test sample concentrations.

The cytotoxicity of CAP, CDPs, FA-CDPs and CAP-FA-CDPs was determined using a 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)− 2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay in a dose dependent and time dependent manner. At first, the HT-29 cells were placed (at a density of 1 × 104 cells/well) in 24-well plates. In a dose dependent study, cells were treated with CAP, CDPs, FA-CDPs and CAP-FA-CDPs. The concentrations of CDPs, FA-CDPs and CAP-FA-CDPs were equivalent to CAP concentrations of 0.05 µg/mL, 0.075 µg/mL, 0.1 µg/mL, 0.5 µg/mL and 1.0 µg/mL. Untreated cells represented the control group. The treatment was first calibrated for 16 h under serum free conditions. PBS was used to rinse the cells and 300 μL of a MTT solution was added into the cells and incubated for a period of 3 h at 37 °C. The number of viable cells was determined by dissolving insoluble formazan crystals using 100 μL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and the absorbance of the resulting purple colored product was determined at 570 nm (Multiskan FC, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Inc., Pittsburgh, PA, USA). In a time dependent study, the cytotoxic effects of CAP-FA-CDPs and CAP were determined at predetermined time intervals of 2, 8, 16, 24 and 36 h, respectively, at a concentration of 0.1 µg/mL (CAP concentration), as selected from the dose dependent study. Here, for CDPs and FA-CDPs, the concentration used was 1.0 µg/mL.

ROS produced intracellularly was determined using an OxiSelect™ Intracellular ROS Assay Kit (DoJindo, Inc, Washington, DC, USA). Cells were subjected to a poly-L-lysine coated glass cover slip at a density of 1 × 104 cells/mL and were incubated for 8 h with CAP, CDPs, FA-CDPs and CAP-FA-CDPs at different concentrations equivalent to CAP concentrations ranging from 0.05 − 1.0 μg/mL. Afterwards, the cells were washed using a Hank’s Balanced Salt solution and then by using a 10 μM dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH- DA) solution at 37 °C while incubating for an hour in the dark. The level of ROS produced in the cells was reflected by the conversion of non-fluorescent DCFH-DA to fluorescent DCFH as indicated by a whole cell bright fluorescent green color. Images were captured and photographed using a confocal microscope with a magnification of 20 X (Leica-SP8, Germany) at excitation and emission wavelengths of 485 and 530 nm, respectively.

To visualize cellular uptake, fluorescence images of the HT-29 cells were obtained. The cells were placed on 24-well culture plates (at a density of 1 × 104 cells/well). Then, one group of these cells was treated with CAP-FA-CDPs conjugates having a CAP concentration of 0.1 µg/mL. The other group of HT-29 cells were treated with CDPs at the same concentration. After incubation for 8 h, the medium was discarded and then the treated groups were rinsed with PBS to remove the unbound samples. Finally, the HT-29 cells were observed under fluorescence microscopy to qualitatively determine the cellular uptake of the developed CDPs and CAP-FA-CDPs delivery system.

HT-29 cells (4 × 104 cells/mL) were cultured in six-well plates and then these cells were incubated with different samples (CDPs, FA-CDPs, CAP and CAP-FA-CDPs) at a fixed concentration (equivalent to a CAP concentration of 0.1 µg/mL) for 8 h. The ‘wild-type’ represents the untreated cells. After the treatment, the cells were cleaned two times with cold PBS. Cells were trypsinized, collected by centrifugation and subsequently incubated with FITC-conjugated Annexin V containing Propidium iodide for 15 min in the dark at room temperature. Afterwards, the incubation apoptotic potential was assessed by a Fluorescence activated cell sorting (FACS) machine (BD Biosciences 7000).

Data obtained through in vitro studies were statistically analyzed either via one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA)- Bonferroni post-test or a two-way ANOVA-Bonferroni post-test using Graph Pad prism 5.0 and values denoted by * (p < 0.05), ** (p < 0.01) and *** (p < 0.001); n = 3, were considered significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the synthesized carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Optical images of the CDs and the CDPs solution were taken in the UV chamber. The CDs and CDPs solutions were yellow in the day light, green under short UV light (254 nm) and exhibited blue fluorescence under long UV light (365 nm) ( Fig. 1). Fluorescence in CDPs is more intense as compared to CDs due to the surface functionalization with PEG6000. Properties like fluorescence, quantum yields, complexation, the color of fluorescence and quenching capability of CDs were greatly affected by surface functionalization [22].

In the photoluminescence spectra, CDPs showed an emission peak with the highest intensity at about 325 nm when excitation was done at 282 nm ( Fig. 2A and B). The PL intensity decreased gradually and a shift in lambda max (λmax) was also observed with a further increase in the excitation wavelength (Fig. 2C). The PL spectra exhibited an excitation dependent emission behavior which is a distinctive characteristic of the CDs [43,44]. PL property of the CDs is mainly attributed to their morphological structure and chemical compositions. Thus, excitation dependent emission behavior observed in the prepared CDs depends on the carbon core nature and / or surface states [45,46].

The UV–visible absorption spectra of CDPs, CAP, FA, and CAP-FA-CDPs are presented in Fig. 3. CDPs exhibited one sharp peak at 230 nm and a shoulder peak at 267 nm as visible in Fig. 3A. The sharp peak arises due to π - π * electronic transitions of carbon-carbon double bonds while the shoulder peak is expected because of n - π * transitions of non-bonding electrons [47,48]. CAP showed two major peaks at 303 nm and 240 nm (Fig. 3B). FA exhibited an absorption peak at 280.5 nm (Fig. 3C). The CAP-FA-CDPs exhibited a broad absorption peak at about 284 nm (Fig. 3D). As in the absorption spectrum of CAP-FA-CDPs, the individual peaks for CAP, FA and CDPs were diminished and a prominent broad peak evolved at a separate wavelength suggesting the conjugation of FA and CAP onto the CDPs. The combined UV spectra of CDPs, CAP, FA, and CAP-FA-CDPs is shown in Fig. 3E.

The surface functionality and chemical nature of the CDs were examined using FTIR and XPS, respectively. FTIR spectra of the biosynthesized CDs, passivated CDs (CDPs), FA conjugated CDPs and CAP-FA-CDPs are presented in Fig. 4.

The peaks for CDs and CDPs were pretty similar. The characteristic broad IR peak centred at 3421.83 cm−1 may be due to the presence of O-H stretching and N-H stretching vibrations. The IR peak at 1693.56 and 1645.33 cm−1 may be because of CO and CC stretching vibrations, respectively. The IR peak at 1506.46 cm−1 may be due to CC (aromatic). The IR peak at 1350.22 cm−1 may be due to C-N stretching (amine) or C-H bending (alkane). The IR peak at 1096.6 cm−1 may be due to C-O stretching (alcohol). Thus, the FTIR spectra of CDs and CDPs suggest the presence of carboxyl and amino functional groups. In the FA-CDPs spectrum (Fig. 4B), the peak at 1700 cm−1 may be for CO of amide and peak at 3264 cm−1 indicates for -NH. Further, peaks at 1112 cm−1 and at 1550 cm−1 may be for C-N stretching and -NH bending, respectively. Thus, the spectrum suggesting the conjugation of FA with CDPs could be via amide bond formation. The attachment of CAP onto FA-CDPs was expected due to electrostatic interactions.
In the XPS scan ( Fig. 5), CDPs was seen to be mainly composed of Carbon (C), Oxygen (O) and traces of Nitrogen (N). Similarly, in the FA-CDPs spectrum, the presence of C, O, and N is mainly noted.

Besides, Na and some inorganic elements were observed in trace amounts. The XPS spectrum of CAP-FA-CDPs also pointed to the presence of C, O and N, with traces of inorganic elements. The presence of inorganic elements may be because of PBS (pH 7.4) which was used as a medium in the conjugation of CAP onto FA-CDPs.

The TEM images revealed the particle size of the CDPs, FA-CDPs and the CAP-FA-CDPs complex ( Fig. 6). CDPs exhibited size less than 10 nm, FA-CDPs exhibited size in the 10–20 nm range and the CAP-FA-CDPs conjugate showed a particle size in the 20–30 nm range. EDX spectra of CDPs (Fig. 6A (right)) suggested the existence of C, N and O as the main elements with traces of other inorganic elements. The EDX analysis of FA-CDPs and CAP-FA-CDPs conjugates as shown in Fig. 6B (right) and C (right) also indicated the existence of C, N and O as the main elements with little inorganic impurities. The results from EDX analysis match with the XPS study results.

The XRD patterns of CDPs and CAP-FA-CDPs are presented in Fig. 7. A single broad peak approximately at 24° in the XRD spectrum of CDPs corresponds to a disordered graphitic structure indicating amorphous nature [49]. Besides, the reported XRD peak of carbon dots lies in between 22° and 25° [26]. The XRD spectrum of capecitabine loaded passivated carbon dots (CAP-FA-CDPs) demonstrated sharp peaks at 33° and at 46°, indicating the crystalline nature of the drug loaded carbon dots. Further, particle size of the carbon dots was estimated with the help of XRD spectra using the Scherrer formula:
Particle size = Kλ / β cos θ
where K= Constant = 0.94; λ = X-ray wavelength; β = peak width; and θ = peak angle. The particle size of the CDPs was estimated to be 1.77 nm whereas the particle size of the capecitabine loaded FA-CDPs was estimated to be 38.28 nm. The particle size obtained through XRD nearly matched with the TEM data.

The zeta potential of the biosynthesized CDs was − 9.73 mV. The negative zeta potential exhibited is the characteristic of a CDs having carboxyl groups [50] which is in accordance with the FTIR data. The zeta potential of capecitabine (CAP) in PBS was − 28.1 mV. Further, the zeta potential of CAP-FA-CDPs was found to be − 15 mV which was due to charge neutralization suggesting the surface conjugation of CAP onto FA-CDPs. Zeta potential is also an indicator of the stability of the nanoparticle system. A greater zeta potential signifies greater charge on nanoparticles surface which prevents their aggregation by repulsion [51]. Thus, the higher negative zeta potential of the CAP-FA-CDPs suggests its stability in solution.

Further, an optical contact angle study was performed to determine the hydrophilicity of the prepared CDs. The contact angle for hydrophilic materials should be in the range of 0–90° [52]. The contact angle of the CDPs was found to be 53°. The measured contact angle suggested good water solubility of CDPs, which is one of the characteristics of CDs [23,53]. The contact angle for the FA-CDPs conjugated system was found to be 55°, which indicated that folic acid conjugation did not affect the hydrophilicity of the passivated CDs. In contrast, the contact angle of the capecitabine conjugated FA-CDPs system was found to be 70°, which was a little higher as compared to the passivated CDs. The images of the contact angle for CDPs, FA-CDPs and CAP-FA-CDPs are shown in Fig. 8.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CAP loading efficiency and loading content
ce:section.ce:para: Using the standard calibration curve of capecitabine (at λmax = 303 nm), the concentration of the unbound capecitabine dialysed outside was calculated. From this, the drug loading efficiency (DLE) and drug loading content (DLC) of the CAP-FA-CDPs conjugate were determined to be 94 ± 1.45% and 1.65 ± 0.12 μg/mg, respectively.

The drug release study for the CAP-FA-CDPs conjugated system was performed at three different pH values which simulate the pH of the endochylema of cancer cells (pH 5.0), the pH of the interstitial fluid (pH 6.8), and the physiological pH (pH 7.4). A pH dependent CAP release profile was observed from the CAP-FA-CDPs conjugate ( Fig. 9A). At pH 5.0 and 6.8, a fast initial CAP release of 42.42% and 37.63% was observed, respectively, followed by a moderate release pattern. After 72 h, 84.57% of CAP was released from the CAP-FA-CDPs conjugate at pH 5.0%, and 67.80% CAP was released at pH 6.8. However, only 24.03% CAP was released at pH 7.4 at the end of 72 h. Thus, the highest CAP release was observed at pH 5.0, which suggested that the CAP-FA-CDPs conjugate has the ability to selectively release drug in the acidic environment of cancer cells. At lower pH (pH 5.0), protonation of the drug CAP is expected to be much higher which may be attributed to the greater drug release [54]. The overall release profile of the CAP-FA-CDPs conjugate indicated that it can provide site selective and sustained drug release.

For the CAP release, at pH 5.0 and pH 6.8, a statistically significant higher release (p < 0.001; n = 3) was seen at pH 5.0 at the 24, 30, 36, 48, 60, and 72 h time points. Similarly, for the comparison of CAP release at pH 5.0 and pH 7.4, a statistically significant higher release (p < 0.001; n = 3) was seen at pH 5.0 at time points between 2 and 72 h (Fig. 9B). Further, statistical analysis of the cumulative drug release at 2 h vs 12 h, 12 h vs 24 h, 24 h vs 48 h, and 48 h vs 72 h showed significant (p < 0.000) differences in drug release over time.

The release profile data was further used to obtain the release kinetics. Zero order, first order, Higuchi, Hixon-Crowell and Korsmeyer-Peppas models were plotted with the help of this release data. The best fit model to explain the release of the capecitabine from the CAP-FA-CDPs conjugate was the Korsmeyer-Peppas model ( Table 1). The n value obtained was 0.53, 0.51 and 0.47 for pH 5.0, 6.8 and 7.4, respectively, which simply indicated that the drug release was through a non-Fickian diffusion process.

The impact of the CDPs, FA-CDPs and CAP-FA-CDPs systems on HT-29 colorectal cancer cell toxicity was studied using MTT assay. The cytotoxicity of HT-29 cells was determined at different concentrations ranging from 0.05 to 1.0 μg/mL ( Fig. 10A) as well as at different time intervals (2–36 h) (Fig. 10B). At the highest concentration used of 1.0 μg/mL, cell viability was above 86% and 81% for CDPs and FA-CDPs, respectively. Likewise, in a time dependent study, after 36 h of incubation, 96.03% and 81.65% of the cells remained viable when treated with CDPs and FA-CDPs, respectively. Thus, it is prominent from such results that CDPs and FA-CDPs did not affect the cell viability that much and possessed negligible toxicity as a carrier material. While for the cytotoxicity assessment of CAP and the CAP-FA-CDPs system at the 0.1 μg/mL concentration, cell viability dropped to 49.03% for CAP and 57.9% for the CAP-FA-CDPs system. Further, when studied over time at the 0.1 μg/mL concentration, the survival percentage of CAP and CAP-FA-CDPs treated cells was only 11.6% and 24.4%, respectively, after 36 h of incubation. In the results, it can be seen that the cytotoxicity of CAP was a little higher than the CAP-FA-CDPs conjugate. However, it is clear from the drug release profile (Fig. 9A) of the CAP-FA-CDPs conjugated system at pH 5.0, that only 71% CAP was released after 36 h, which suggests less exposure of CAP in the case of the conjugated system treated group as compared to the CAP treated group.

Further, it was observed that after the exposure of CAP and CAP-FA-CDPs for 16 h, the percentage of cell viability was 29.1% and 33.3%, respectively. However, in the case of CAP-FA-CDPs after 16 h, the drug release was only 45% (Fig. 9A) as compared to CAP which was fully accessible to show cytotoxicity in the HT-29 cells. Accordingly, it is understandable that the cytotoxicity of the CAP-FA-CDPs conjugate towards HT-29 cells was significantly greater than the CAP only. This higher toxicity of CAP-FA-CDPs as compared to CAP may be attributed to the greater internalization of the conjugated system via folate mediated receptors expressed by malignant cells.

Further, the IC50 value of the CAP-FA-CDPs system was calculated and compared with reported nano formulations developed for CAP delivery in colorectal cancer ( Table 2). The IC50 value of the CAP-FA-CDPs system was 0.73 µg/mL in a colon cancer cell line and it was significantly lesser than earlier reported chitosan glucuronic acid conjugate coated CAP loaded mesoporous silica nanoparticles (IC50 = 42.04 µg/mL) by Narayan et al.[55] and CAP loaded solid lipid nanoparticles (IC50 = 52 µg/mL, approx.) by Dudhipala and Puchchakayala [15]. The significantly lower IC50 value for the currently developed CAP-FA-CDPs nano delivery system suggests its superiority as a suitable nanocarrier for CAP delivery in colorectal cancer.

When ROS level inside a cancer cell increases, it produces oxidative stress which ultimately causes cell apoptosis [56]. Here, the effect of CAP, CDPs, FA-CDPs and CAP-FA-CDPs on the ROS level of HT-29 cancer cells was evaluated after implementing a DCFH-DA dye-based assay method. The DCFH-DA dye enters inside the cell through passive uptake and then gets converted into fluorescent DCFH in the presence of ROS. The level of ROS generated inside a cell is reflected by the fluorescence intensity of the DCFH dye. As shown in Fig. 11, the fluorescence intensity of CDPs and FA-CDPs remained almost unaffected with increasing concentrations suggesting no or minimal ROS production. However, CAP-FA-CDPs and CAP showed an increase in fluorescence intensity with increasing concentrations.

The fluorescent intensity of CAP-FA-CDPs treated cells was higher than CAP treated cells, which may be due to the attachment of FA which facilitate cellular uptake and subsequently more ROS generation. Similarly, as compared to CDPs, at higher concentrations (0.1–1.0 µg/mL), a significant increase in fluorescence intensity was noted for the CAP-FA-CDPs (***p < 0.001; n = 3), suggesting a meaningful ROS generation inside HT-29 cancer cells. Further, a statistical comparison (one way ANOVA- Bonferroni post-test) of fluorescent intensity generated by the CAP-FA-CDPs treated group showed significant increases (p < 0.0001) in intensity at 1.0 µg/mL when compared to 0.05 µg/mL. The developed fluorescence in HT-29 cells at a 0.1 µg/mL concentration was captured using confocal microscopy ( Fig. 12).

To analyze the cellular uptake of the CAP-FA-CDPs conjugate in comparison with CDPs, HT-29 cells were treated with the CAP-FA-CDPs conjugate and CDPs for 8 h, followed by fluorescence imaging. The visible blue fluorescence emitted from the CAP-FA-CDPs treated cells ( Fig. 13A) suggests significant cellular internalization of the conjugate. This cellular uptake was mediated through the interaction between folic acids (FAs) decorated on the surface of the conjugate and folate receptors overexpressed on the cancer cell surface. In contrast, a negligible fluorescence was observed for the CDPs treated cells (Fig. 13B) which indicates minimal cellular uptake in the absence of FA.

Flow cytometry was performed to analyze the impact of CAP, CDPs, FA-CDPs and CAP-FA-CDPs in the apoptosis of HT-29 cells. The percentage of live cells (Q1), early apoptotic (Q2), late apoptotic (Q3) and necrotic cells (Q4) found after treatment is presented in Fig. 14. The percentage of live cells in the wild type (untreated group), CDPs and FA-CDPs treated cells was similar and above 80%. This confirmed the minimal cytotoxicity of the present carrier material CDPs, and even also when conjugated with FA (FA-CDPs). The live cell percentages in the CAP-FA-CDPs group was significantly decreased at 21.4%, and apoptotic cells were 77.2%. In the CAP group, the live cell percentage remained at 72.4% and the apoptototic cells count was only 27.4%. This could be because of the much greater cellular uptake of the CAP-FA-CDPs conjugate in the presence of the targeting ligand (FA), showing significant apoptotic potential as compared to CAP. Thus, the flow cytometry results confirmed the better targeting ability and apoptotic potential of the presently developed CAP-FA-CDPs conjugated delivery system.

In this present work, passivated carbon dots (CDPs) were synthesized using a microwave method with the Buchnania lanzan leaf extract as a precursor and PEG6000 as a passivating agent. Folic acid was successfully anchored on the CDPs surface through the sonication method. CAP was loaded on the CDPs surface through electrostatic interactions to finally obtain CAP-FA-CDPs. The characterizations of CDPs, FA-CDPs and CAP-FA-CDPs were carried out via various analytical methods like fluorescence spectroscopy, UV-Visible spectroscopy, FTIR, XPS, TEM/EDX, OCA, and XRD. The CAP-FA-CDPs conjugate possessed a high drug loading efficiency at 94 ± 1.45%. Maximum drug release from the CAP-FA-CDPs system was seen in the weakly acidic environment of pH 5.0, in a sustained release manner after an initial burst release. The in vitro cytotoxicity study suggested a minimal cytotoxic nature of the carrier material CDPs and FA-CDPs towards HT-29 cells after 36 h of treatment at a concentration of 1.0 μg/mL. In contrast, CAP-FA-CDPs showed significant cytotoxicity towards HT-29 colorectal cancer cells after the same treatment time period and at a concentration of 0.1 μg/mL. A cellular uptake study confirmed the FA mediated internalization of the CAP-FA-CDPs conjugate into HT-29 cells. A ROS study further supported the obtained results. CAP-FA-CDPs displayed significant fluorescence due to the generation of free radicals after entry into the cells. Finally, the flow cytometry results established the non-cytotoxic nature of the carrier material CDPs and significant apoptotic potential of the CAP-FA-CDPs. In a nutshell, the newly developed CAP-FA-CDPs system displayed high selectivity and excellent antitumor activity, which can serve as an advanced nano-delivery system in the cancer treatment.

Shivangi Paliwal: Data curation, Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis. Pradip Jana: Investigation, Methodology, writing – original draft. Sneha Singh: Writing – review & editing. Harishkumar Madhyastha: Methodology, Formal analysis Thomas Webster: Writing – review & editing. Abhimanyu Dev: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Supervision, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Pradip Jana would like to acknowledge Indian Council of Medical Research (ICMR), New Delhi, India for providing a Senior Research Fellowship (SRF) (Award letter file no. 5/3/8/26/ITR-F/2022).
